explanation: This LTL property expresses the following: Whenever the system is in either PCV mode or PSV mode and the Expiratory Pause is requested by the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton), then starting from the next state, wait until the next occurrence of expiratoryPhaseEnd (i.e., the end of the next expiration phase). If, at that moment, the request is still active (expiratoryPauseButton), then the ventilator enters the Expiratory Pause state (expiratoryPause, inValveClose, outValveClose), and must remain so as long as the request remains active. Once the request is cleared (¬expiratoryPauseButton), the Expiratory Pause state can end. This captures the input requirements by chaining the explicit trigger (button press), waiting for the synchronizing event (expiratoryPhaseEnd), and then latching Expiratory Pause behavior until GUI releases it. The previous property was not well-formed due to parentheses and trying to conflate guarantees for both waiting and latching phases inside one nested G. The new property splits the behavior into two clearly sequenced U (until) formulas: one for waiting for the right moment, and one for keeping Expiratory Pause until stop. This fixes the LTL syntax as all parentheses and scopes are now valid and correct.
output_LTL: G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton) -> X((!(expiratoryPhaseEnd) U (expiratoryPhaseEnd & expiratoryPauseButton & ((expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose) U !expiratoryPauseButton)))))

explanation: The input requirement states that when the Expiratory Pause is set by the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton), the ventilator waits until the end of the current expiration phase, then, if the pause is still required (expiratoryPause), it will close both inspiratory and expiratory valves (inValveClose & outValveClose) until the pause is stopped by the GUI (¬expiratoryPause). This behavior should apply only in PCV or PSV modes. Thus, the LTL formula says: Whenever we are in either PCVMode or PSVMode and the Expiratory Pause is requested via the GUI, from the next end of expiration (expiratoryPhaseEnd), if the pause is still required, both valves must be closed (inValveClose & outValveClose) until the pause request is cleared. This matches the structured phases described in the input.
output_LTL: G ( (PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton -> X ( (F expiratoryPhaseEnd) -> (expiratoryPause U ( (inValveClose & outValveClose) U (!expiratoryPause) ) ) ) )

explanation: The natural language requirement specifies that whenever in PCV or PSV mode and the expiratory pause has been requested via the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton), on the next expiration phase end, if the pause is still requested, both valves (inValveClose & outValveClose) should be closed and remain closed until the pause is stopped by the GUI. This is formalized by stating: whenever we are in PCV or PSV mode and the expiratory pause button is pressed, then after the end of the next expiration phase, if the button is still held, both valves must be closed globally (i.e., until the button is released). The LTL provided ensures this causality and mapping from the scenario description.
output_LTL: G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton & X F expiratoryPhaseEnd) -> X (expiratoryPauseButton -> G((inValveClose & outValveClose) W !expiratoryPauseButton)))

explanation: This LTL property captures the core requirement: When the Expiratory Pause is set by the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton becomes true), and if the system is in PCV or PSV mode (PCVMode | PSVMode), then after the next expiration phase completes (expiratoryPhaseEnd), the ventilator should close both valves (inValveClose & outValveClose)—but only if the Expiratory Pause is still active (expiratoryPause remains true). This state of both valves remaining closed continues until Expiratory Pause is stopped by the GUI (expiratoryPause becomes false). The property models this temporally: whenever the GUI sets the Expiratory Pause, it is required that after the next expiration ends, if Expiratory Pause is still in effect, both valves are closed until the signal is cleared. Previous attempts often forget to temporally link the button press to the next expiration phase end and the sustained valve state, but this property addresses that.
output_LTL: G((expiratoryPauseButton & (PCVMode | PSVMode)) -> (F(expiratoryPhaseEnd & expiratoryPause & X(G((expiratoryPause) -> (inValveClose & outValveClose)) U (!expiratoryPause)))))

explanation: The requirement states that in PCV or PSV mode, when the expiratory pause is requested via GUI, after the next expiration phase completes, if the pause is still required, both the inspiratory and expiratory valves shall close and remain closed until the GUI stops the pause. There are no atomic propositions for 'PCV or PSV mode' besides 'PCVMode' and 'PSVMode'. The expiratory pause command is represented by 'expiratoryPauseButton' and actual pause by 'expiratoryPause'. Valve states are 'inValveClose' and 'outValveClose'. The LTL formula uses the basic structure: whenever the system is in PCV or PSV mode and the expiratory pause is requested by GUI ('expiratoryPauseButton'), then after the end of the next expiration phase ('expiratoryPhaseEnd') and if the pause is still required ('expiratoryPauseButton'), then eventually ('F') both valves should close ('inValveClose' & 'outValveClose') and stay closed until the pause is stopped ('!expiratoryPauseButton'). This is best captured by an implication, a conditional 'until', and correct sequencing: once the request button is pressed, after the next expiration end, if the request is still active, the closure is enforced until the button is released.
output_LTL: G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton) -> (X(F(expiratoryPhaseEnd & expiratoryPauseButton) -> (F((inValveClose & outValveClose) U (!expiratoryPauseButton))))))

explanation: This LTL property captures the requirement that, in PCV or PSV mode, if an expiratory pause is requested by the user through the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton), then, after the next expiration phase ends (expiratoryPhaseEnd), if the pause is still required (expiratoryPause remains true), both the inspiratory and expiratory valves must be closed (inValveClose & outValveClose) until the pause is ended (i.e., until not expiratoryPause). To capture this, we use the following translation: Whenever we are in PCVMode or PSVMode, and expiratoryPauseButton becomes true, after the next occurrence of expiratoryPhaseEnd, if expiratoryPause holds, then from that point, until not expiratoryPause, both inValveClose and outValveClose must be true. This property reflects the described sequence and ensures the valves remain closed during the pause, and only until the pause is stopped. Previous properties might miss: (1) waiting for expiration to finish after the pause is set, (2) the requirement that both valves must stay closed specifically during the pause state, (3) ensuring this is limited to PCV and PSV modes, and (4) re-checking that the pause is still desired before closing the valves, as the request could be withdrawn before reaching expiration end.
output_LTL: G( (PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton -> F( expiratoryPhaseEnd & (expiratoryPause -> ((inValveClose & outValveClose) U !expiratoryPause)) ) )

explanation: The natural language requires that (1) in PCV or PSV modes, if Expiratory Pause is set by the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton) during expiration, then after the next expiration phase ends, the ventilator closes both inspiratory and expiratory valves (inValveClose & outValveClose) until the pause is stopped. The atomic propositions do not explicitly encode 'expiratoryPause stopped by GUI', so we must conservatively use 'not expiratoryPauseButton' for release. The requirement is conditional on PCV or PSV Mode being active, hence the trigger. The LTL below expresses: 'Globally, if in PCV or PSV mode, and during expiration, the Expiratory Pause is set by the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton), then after the next expiration phase ends (first X(expiratoryPhaseEnd)), if Expiratory Pause is still required (expiratoryPauseButton still true), then both valves will close (inValveClose & outValveClose) and remain so until the pause is stopped (i.e., as long as Expiratory Pause is still required).'
output_LTL: G( (PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPhase & expiratoryPauseButton -> X( ( !expiratoryPhaseEnd U (expiratoryPhaseEnd & X( expiratoryPauseButton -> ( (inValveClose & outValveClose) V !expiratoryPauseButton ) ) ) ) ) )

explanation:
The requirement states that, in PCV and PSV modes, if an Expiratory Pause is requested/set by the GUI (captured by 'expiratoryPauseButton'), then after the current (or next) expiration phase ends ('expiratoryPhaseEnd'), if the pause is still requested, both inspiratory and expiratory valves must close (inValveClose and outValveClose), and these valves remain closed until the Expiratory Pause is stopped (expiratoryPauseButton no longer true). We encode that: whenever we are in PCVMode or PSVMode, and expiratoryPauseButton is true, then following the end of the expiration phase, the valves should close and stay closed as long as the Expiratory Pause is still requested. This LTL property ensures this behavior. It does not attempt to capture temporal distance or counting expiratory phases, since only atomic propositions are allowed (no arithmetic/phase counting).

Previous LTL encodings (if any) may not explicitly synchronize the closing of both valves to the first expiration phase end after the Expiratory Pause is set, nor ensure that as long as the pause is set, both valves remain closed. This LTL directly links these conditions.
output_LTL: G( ((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton) -> F( expiratoryPhaseEnd & X( (expiratoryPauseButton) U (!expiratoryPauseButton & inValveClose & outValveClose) ) ) )

explanation: The specification requires that in PCV or PSV modes, when an Expiratory Pause is set by the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton), the ventilator waits for the end of the next expiration phase (expiratoryPhaseEnd), and if Expiratory Pause is still required (expiratoryPauseButton remains true), both valves remain closed (inValveClose & outValveClose) until the pause is stopped by the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton becomes false). The LTL property below expresses this logic: whenever (globally) we are in PCVMode or PSVMode and the Expiratory Pause is requested (expiratoryPauseButton), eventually after the next expiratoryPhaseEnd, if the button is still pressed, both valves must stay closed until it is released. The property does not use numerical comparisons and stays strictly within the given atomic propositions and logic.
output_LTL: G ((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton -> F (expiratoryPhaseEnd & expiratoryPauseButton & X ( (inValveClose & outValveClose) U !expiratoryPauseButton ) ) )

explanation: This property expresses that whenever Expiratory Pause is set by the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton), and the ventilator is in PCV or PSV mode (PCVMode or PSVMode), then following the next end of expiration (expiratoryPhaseEnd), if the Expiratory Pause is still requested at that point, both valves (inValveClose and outValveClose) must be closed as long as the Expiratory Pause continues, and remain so until the pause is stopped (expiratoryPauseButton becomes false). The formula maintains proper LTL syntax by ensuring the logic is a global implication leading from the button being set, through the first matching event, into the enforced state, and only releases it when Expiratory Pause is unset.
output_LTL: G ( ((PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton & X(expiratoryPhaseEnd)) -> X ( (expiratoryPauseButton) -> ((inValveClose & outValveClose) U !expiratoryPauseButton) ) )
